frameworks.name: pupillometry methodology
frameworks.one_line: Pupil size as a physiological readout of cognitive state.

frameworks.name: probabilistic-inference de-confounding
frameworks.one_line: Statistical model that infers and removes blink-evoked pupil artifacts.

core_question: Does eye blinking, through its cognitively-dependent occurrence, confound pupillary measurements, and can it be statistically removed?
key_assumptions: Blinks are not random but co-vary with cognitive components
Blink-locked pupillary response (BPR) is a stereotyped pulse with characterizable shape
Confounds can be modelled as additive components on raw pupil signals
manipulations: Characterization of BPR shape and magnitude across subjects and blinks
Application of the proposed de-confounding algorithm versus raw pupillometry
key_findings: Eye blinks induce a pulse-like pupillary change (constriction then dilation) with substantial magnitude and duration
Blink occurrence depends on cognitive factors, producing BPR-mediated confounds in pupillary measures
The proposed algorithm selectively removes BPR and increases statistical power of pupillometry
interpretations: Cognitive dependence of blinking can systematically distort pupillometric inferences unless explicitly modelled
Probabilistic de-confounding is a viable remedy that preserves cognitively-driven pupil signals
